2: A fulfillment: 'YHWH said to Moses, Tell Aaron your brother not to come at any time into the Holy Place inside the veil, before the mercy seat that is on the ark, so that he may not die. For I will appear in the cloud over the mercy seat.' The entire Yom Kippur ritual — its restrictions, its blood-sprinkling, its once-per-year access, its careful approach protocol — is the OT's most detailed type of Christ's high-priestly work. Hebrews 9:1-14 maps the Yom Kippur elements onto Christ systematically: the holy of holies → heaven; the high priest → Christ; bull and goat blood → Christ's own blood; annual repetition → once for all; the mercy seat → Christ as hilasterion (Romans 3:25). Every element of Leviticus 16 is the shadow; Christ is the substance.
1: After the death of Aaron's two sons when they drew near before YHWH — Yom Kippur's legislation is framed by the deaths of Nadab and Abihu (Leviticus 10:1-3): unauthorized approach to the most holy place results in death. This death-framing establishes the theological premise for the entire chapter: the problem of human approach to the holy God. Christ answers this problem not by heightening the restriction (as Yom Kippur's annual once-only limitation does) but by permanently resolving it: 'Therefore, brothers, since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus' (Hebrews 10:19).
3: Aaron shall come into the Holy Place with a young bull for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering — the high priest enters with specific prescribed animals. Hebrews 7:27 identifies the contrast: 'He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people, since he did this once for all when he offered up himself.' Aaron's required bull for his own sins is the type that reveals Christ's superiority: the perfect High Priest needs no personal sin offering because he has no sin to atone for.
4: He shall put on the holy linen coat and shall have the linen undergarments on his body — on Yom Kippur the high priest strips the gold and jewels and wears only white linen. The voluntary self-reduction to the simplest priestly garments on the day of atonement is the type of Christ's kenosis: 'who, though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, taking the form of a servant' (Philippians 2:6-7). The glory is not abandoned but veiled; the linen covers the gold as the human nature veils the divine glory.
5: From the congregation two male goats for a sin offering — the two-goat complex is a single sin offering with a split function: one goat dies, one carries sin away. Together they accomplish what no single sacrifice mechanism can express: the removal of sin's guilt (through death and blood) and the removal of sin's presence (through the carrying-away to the wilderness). Christ fulfills both simultaneously: 'the Lamb of God who takes away (airōn) the sin of the world' (John 1:29) — takes away by bearing and removing, not merely covering.
6: Aaron shall offer the bull as a sin offering for himself — the high priest atones for himself before atoning for the congregation. Christ inverts this sequence: he has no sin to atone for, so his atoning work is entirely on behalf of others from the first moment. 'For we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin' (Hebrews 4:15). The absence of a personal sin offering for Christ is the proof of his priestly perfection.
7: He shall take the two goats and set them before YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the two goats presented together as a single offering unit. Christ is the complete fulfillment of both goats simultaneously: his death satisfies divine justice (YHWH-lot goat), and his resurrection carries sin away permanently (Azazel-lot goat). The separation of the two functions in the type is resolved in the single act of the antitype: 'He has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Hebrews 9:26).
8: One lot for YHWH and one lot for Azazel — the divine determination of which goat fulfills which function. Christ's appointment as the sacrifice was not contingent or accidental: 'this Jesus, delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God' (Acts 2:23); 'a lamb without blemish or spot who was foreknown before the foundation of the world' (1 Peter 1:19-20). The lot that falls before YHWH is the type of the eternal election of the Son as both YHWH-lot and Azazel-lot offering.
9: The goat on which the lot fell for YHWH shall be used as a sin offering — the slaughtered goat, the one offered to YHWH, provides the blood for the sanctuary's purification. Christ's blood is specifically the purifying agent for the heavenly sanctuary: 'it was necessary for the copies of the heavenly things to be purified with these rites, but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices than these' (Hebrews 9:23). The YHWH-lot goat's blood entering the most holy place is the type of Christ's own blood entering the heavenly sanctuary.
10: The goat on which the lot fell for Azazel shall be presented alive before YHWH to make atonement over it — the Azazel goat is presented alive, not yet sent, while Aaron confesses over it. The living goat bears confession before being sent. Christ also lives — is presented before the Father as the living Lord — while his sacrifice accomplished the bearing-away of sin. 'Christ Jesus is the one who died — more than that, who was raised — who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us' (Romans 8:34). He is the Azazel-goat still present, alive, interceding.
11: Aaron shall present the bull as a sin offering for himself — the second mention of Aaron's personal sin offering. The repetition underlines the contrast with Christ: the Levitical high priest must atone for himself twice (Leviticus 16:6 and 11); Christ atones for himself zero times. Every mention of Aaron's personal atonement requirement is an implicit pointer to its absence in the perfect High Priest. 'Such a high priest was indeed fitting for us: holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens' (Hebrews 7:26).
12: A censer full of coals from the altar and two handfuls of incense to create a cloud covering the mercy seat — the incense protects Aaron from the unveiled divine presence. Christ enters the heavenly most holy place not with incense for protection but with his own blood that gives him free access: 'by the greater and more perfect tent... not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, he entered once for all into the holy places, securing an eternal redemption' (Hebrews 9:11-12). The protection the incense provided is rendered unnecessary by the access Christ's blood secured.
13: The incense cloud shall cover the mercy seat so that Aaron does not die — the protective cloud between the priest and the divine presence. Christ needs no such protection: 'he always lives in the presence of God' (Hebrews 9:24, emfanisthēnai tō prosōpō tou theou — to appear in the presence of God). The Son who is 'the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature' (Hebrews 1:3) faces the divine presence directly without mediating cloud.
14: Some blood of the bull sprinkled on the front of the mercy seat seven times — the blood manipulation on the kapporet. Hebrews 9:7,11-12: 'into the second tent only the high priest goes, and only once a year, and not without blood... Christ entered once for all into the holy places... by means of his own blood, securing an eternal redemption.' Every element of Leviticus 16:14 is explicitly contrasted in Hebrews: Aaron enters with someone else's blood, once a year, for temporary coverage; Christ enters with his own blood, once for all time, for eternal redemption.
15: He shall kill the goat of the sin offering that is for the people and bring its blood inside the veil — the communal sin offering's blood entering the most holy place. Christ enters the heavenly sanctuary not with the blood of goats but with his own blood as the communal sin offering for all humanity: 'he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (1 John 2:2). The communal scope of the Yom Kippur goat's blood — covering all Israel — points to the universal scope of Christ's atoning blood.
16: Atonement for the Holy Place because of the uncleannesses of the people — the sanctuary itself requires annual purification from accumulated human impurity. Christ's sacrifice purifies the conscience from dead works: 'how much more will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God, purify our conscience from dead works to serve the living God' (Hebrews 9:14). The sanctuary-purification of the type becomes the conscience-purification of the antitype.
17: No one may be in the tent of meeting during the high priest's atoning work — absolute solitude in the most critical priestly moment. In Gethsemane and on the cross, Christ is ultimately alone: 'they all left him and fled' (Mark 14:50); 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' (Mark 15:34). The required solitude of the Yom Kippur high priest is fulfilled in the desolate solitude of the crucified High Priest who bears the full weight of atonement without human support.
18: He shall go out to the outer altar and put blood from both offerings on its horns — after the inner sanctuary, the outer altar receives blood from both the priestly and communal sin offerings. The mixed blood (bull + goat) on the altar represents the complete covering of both the priestly and lay dimensions of the community's sin. Christ's blood covers the full spectrum: 'This is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins' (Matthew 26:28). 'Many' (pollōn) translates the Isaiah 53:12 rabbim — the comprehensive covenant community.
19: He shall sprinkle the blood on the outer altar seven times and cleanse and consecrate it — seven sprinklings reconsecrate the altar that has been defiled by the year's accumulated sin. Christ's blood reconsecrates fallen humanity itself: 'you are a chosen race, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, a people for his own possession' (1 Peter 2:9). The altar cleansed and reconsecrated by Yom Kippur blood is the type of the royal priesthood cleansed and consecrated by Christ's blood to serve the living God.
20: When he has finished atoning for the Holy Place and the tent and the altar, he shall present the live goat — the spatial sequence of atonement ends with the live Azazel goat. After Christ's sacrifice (the YHWH-lot completion), he presents himself alive as the Azazel-dimension: the risen Christ who carries human sin away permanently, seated at the right hand of the Father (Hebrews 1:3; Leviticus 10:12). The presentation of the live goat after the atoning work is the type of the resurrection-presentation of the risen Lord who continues to bear and remove sin through his perpetual intercession.
21: Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat and confess all the iniquities and transgressions and sins of Israel — the most explicit sin-bearing act in the OT, preceding Isa 53's language: the full weight of Israel's sin is transferred to the goat through the double-hand gesture and verbal confession. Isaiah 53:6: 'YHWH has laid on him the iniquity of us all' (hifgea bo, caused to fall upon him). 2 Corinthians 5:21: 'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin.' The Azazel goat's sin-loading is fulfilled in the cross where all human iniquity is laid upon the Son.
22: The goat shall bear all their iniquities to a remote area and be let go — nasa (bear, carry, remove) is the verb used for the Servant's sin-bearing in Isaiah 53:4,11,12: 'he has borne our griefs... he bore the sin of many.' The scapegoat going to the remote cut-off land is the type of Christ's 'descent into the lower parts of the earth' (Ephesians 4:9), bearing sin into the domain of death and abandonment, and of his permanent removal of sin: 'as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us' (Psalms 103:12).
23: Aaron shall come into the tent of meeting and take off the linen garments he put on when he went into the Holy Place — after the atonement work is complete, the high priest removes the linen and (Leviticus 16:24) resumes the gold vestments. Christ's humiliation is followed by his exaltation: 'therefore God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name' (Philippians 2:9). The resuming of the regular high-priestly garments after Yom Kippur is the type of the resurrection-glorification: the servant-form of the linen gives way to the royal-form of the gold.
24: He shall bathe his body in water in a holy place and put on his garments and come out and offer his burnt offering and the burnt offering of the people — the post-atonement purification, change of garments, and resumption of normal priestly ministry. Christ's resurrection is the post-atonement return: he comes out of the tomb having completed the atoning work, resumes his royal-priestly ministry, and presents the burnt offering of his complete consecration to the Father ('it is finished' → the ascension where he presents himself as the final offering, Hebrews 9:24).
25: The fat of the sin offerings burned on the altar — even on Yom Kippur the fat belongs to YHWH. The perpetual fat-offering on the altar on the holiest day of the year confirms that what belongs to God cannot be withheld even in the most intensive human atonement act. Christ's life 'poured out' on the cross (Matthew 20:28; Philippians 2:17: 'poured out as a drink offering') is the total giving of the divine-human richness to the Father, the ultimate fat-offering on the altar of the cross.
26: He who led the goat to Azazel shall wash his clothes and bathe — the sin-laden goat's handler contracts impurity. The disciples who witnessed the crucifixion — who watched the one bearing all human sin — needed the resurrection to make sense of what they had witnessed; the proximity to the sin-bearing Jesus in his death created a kind of moral-spiritual disorientation that only the resurrection-encounter could 'purify.' Peter's reinstatement (John 21:15-19) is the type of the washing-after-scapegoat-handling that the resurrection provided for those who were near the cross.
27: The sin offering animals whose blood was brought into the Holy Place shall be burned outside the camp — the outside-camp burning of the most-holy sin offering is the direct type cited in Hebrews 13:11-13: 'For the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood. Therefore let us go to him outside the camp and bear the reproach he endured.' The type is explicitly applied: Golgotha is the outside-camp burning location of the perfect sin offering.
28: He who burns them shall wash his clothes and bathe — the contact with the most holy sin offering carcasses creates impurity for the handler, requiring purification. All who participate in Christ's atoning death — all who are baptized into his death (Romans 6:3) — undergo the washing-and-bathing transformation: 'you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God' (1 Corinthians 6:11). The purification required of those who handled the sin offering becomes the washing given to those who come through Christ's death.
29: On the tenth day of the seventh month you shall afflict yourselves and do no work — Yom Kippur's communal self-affliction creates corporate participation in the high-priestly atonement; the people join the suffering dimension of the high priest's solitary work through their fast. Christ's call to 'deny yourself and take up your cross' (Mark 8:34) extends the Yom Kippur self-affliction pattern into continuous discipleship: the annual fast becomes the daily self-denial of the new covenant community.
30: On this day atonement shall be made for you to cleanse you — mikal chatoteichem lifney YHWH titharu, from all your sins before YHWH you shall be clean. The comprehensive atonement of Yom Kippur — 'all your sins' — is the type of the total justification provided by Christ: 'there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus' (Romans 8:1). The annual declaration of total cleanliness becomes the permanent verdict of justification in Christ: 'God presented him as the one who would turn aside his wrath, taking away sin' (Romans 3:25 NIV).
31: Shabbat shabbaton — the most intense Sabbath rest, the Sabbath of Sabbaths. Yom Kippur's super-Sabbath is the type of the rest secured by Christ's completed atonement: 'whoever has entered God's rest has also rested from his works as God did from his' (Hebrews 4:10). Christ's 'It is finished' (John 19:30) is the declaration of the ultimate shabbat shabbaton — the rest from the works of atonement, the completed work that creates the basis for the eschatological Sabbath-rest of the people of God.
32: The anointed priest who serves in his father's place shall perform the atonement — the successor high priest performs the identical ceremony; the rite is perpetuated through the Aaronic succession. Christ's priesthood is not succeeded but permanent: 'he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever' (Hebrews 7:24). The Levitical succession that required each generation to repeat the atonement is contrasted with the permanent, non-successive priesthood of the Son whose single atonement requires no successor and no repetition.
33: He shall make atonement for the holy sanctuary, the tent, the altar, the priests, and all the people — the comprehensive four-part atonement: sacred spaces and sacred persons. Christ's atonement covers the full scope of what Yom Kippur addressed and more: 'it was necessary for the copies of the heavenly things to be purified with these rites, but the heavenly things themselves with better sacrifices' (Hebrews 9:23). The range of what the Levitical atonement covered is precisely the range of what Christ's sacrifice definitively covers — and the heavenly originals beyond.
34: This shall be a statute forever: once a year atonement shall be made for all the sins of Israel — achat bashanah, once per year. The once-per-year restriction is Hebrews' primary evidence for the inadequacy of the Levitical system and the necessity of Christ's once-for-all sacrifice: 'But when Christ appeared as a high priest of the good things that have come... he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, securing an eternal redemption' (Hebrews 9:11-12). Nor is it repeated annually any longer: 'he has appeared once for all at the end of the ages to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself' (Hebrews 9:26).
